---
title: "Draft: Intro/Conclusions"
---
## Introduction

Workplace injuries are a significant concern for businesses across all industries, costing
employers billions of dollars annually in lost productivity, medical expenses, and workers'
compensation claims. Falls in particular are one of the leading causes of occupational injury
in the United States. A recent article raised concern that office workers may be more likely
to be injured through falls than non office workers, prompting our company's safety officer
to question whether stronger safety policies are needed for office staff. To evaluate this
claim, this report uses data from the BLS (Bureau of Labor Statistics) Survey of
Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII) [@IIFDatabasesUS] to compare injury and fall
rates across office and non office industries. A two sample t-test is used to assess
whether differences in same level fall rates between the two groups are statistically
significant.

## Conclusion

The analysis found that office workers experience significantly fewer same level fall
injuries than non office workers (t(3.5) = -3.64, p = 0.027), with office industries
averaging 6.5 fall cases per 10,000 FTE workers compared to 15.7 for non office industries.
Overall injury rates follow the same pattern, with office industries reporting total
recordable case (TRC) rates well below both non office industries and the private industry
average. Taken together, the evidence does not support implementing stronger fall safety
policies specifically for office workers. Instead, the company's safety resources would be
better directed toward its industrial and manufacturing operations where risk is
substantially higher. Future work could examine injury severity more closely using days away
from work data, or explore whether specific office occupations such as facilities or
mailroom staff carry elevated risk compared to fully desk based roles.
